name: Python assignment
instructions:
STEPS
What is a Program?



A program is a set of instructions that a computer follows to complete a task. The computer receives the information, handles it, utilizes it, and then gives an output based on it by following a program.We are surrounded by computers and computer programs; phones or tablets you can play or watch videos on. Other example are traffic lights and they follow different programs to carry out different tasks.



Let's See How Programs Work!

When you want to play your favorite game, like "Minecraft" or "Roblox," you tap its picture, right? A special program inside the tablet quickly makes the game start for you! It's like the tablet knows exactly how to open the game because of those instructions.



Another example is that when you're in a car, you see traffic lights turn red, then yellow, then green. They don't just change randomly! A program inside the traffic light tells it exactly when to turn red (so cars stop) and when to turn green (so cars go).



According to Linda Liukas, a Finnish computer programmer, children’s writer, and programming instructor, programming consists of three parts:Planning the programWriting the program in a programming language into a codeTesting and debugging the program







The second part is what everybody generally considers programming. Only this part requires coding skills; the first and third parts require additional skills like problem-solving, and decomposition (the ability to break down bigger problems into smaller, manageable tasks).



Programming AnalogyGetting Dressed

On days that you have to go to school, your mother and father are behind you in the morning, asking you to wake up, while you keep saying ‘5 more minutes!’ until you realize it is late and you don’t have time to take bath. So, you decide to just wash your face, brush your teeth, and get dressed. To get dressed, you follow a particular sequence of steps, like the one given below:

Take off the pajamas

name: Introduction to Robotics Quiz (Kids Level)
description: This quiz helps learners understand the basics of robotics, including robot components, movement, sensors, and simple logic. It is designed for beginners to test their foundational knowledge in a fun and engaging way.
instructions:
Instructions:

Read each question carefully.
Choose the correct answer from the options provided.
Each question has only one correct answer.
Select the best answer and proceed to the next question.
Try your best and have fun!

items.question: 1. What is a robot?
options: B. A machine that can perform tasks automatically
C. A toy only used for playing

name: Robot Parts Labeling Assignment
description: Learners identify and describe the function of key robot components in a short written and diagram-based assignment.
instructions:
Instructions:

Label each part of the robot diagram provided.
Write one sentence explaining what each part does.
Submit your labeled diagram and written answers together.
Use full sentences and correct spelling where possible.

rubric.description: All robot parts are correctly identified and labeled.
Each part's function is explained clearly and correctly.
Diagram and answers are neat, organized, and easy to follow.
All required parts and questions are fully attempted.

name: Build-a-Simple-Robot Project
description: A hands-on project where learners design, build, and test a simple robot using provided materials, applying concepts of movement, sensors, and basic logic.
instructions:
Instructions:

Work individually or in small groups to design your robot.
Build your robot using the provided kit or materials.
Test that your robot performs its intended task.
Present your robot and explain how it works to the class.

rubric.description: Robot design is thoughtful and matches the stated goal.
Robot is built securely and components are properly connected.
Robot successfully performs its intended task.
Learner clearly explains how the robot works and the choices made.
Learner collaborates well and shows consistent effort throughout.

name: Robotics Classroom Behavior Observation
description: An observational checklist used by the teacher to assess a learner's engagement, collaboration, and problem-solving behavior during robotics activities.
instructions:
Instructions:

Observe the learner during the robotics session.
Rate each indicator based on what you observe, not what the learner says.
Use the rating scale consistently across all learners.
Add notes separately if needed for report comments.

indicators.text: Collaborates respectfully with peers during group tasks
Attempts to troubleshoot problems before asking for help
Stays engaged and on-task throughout the session

name: Robotics Fundamentals Quiz
description: A mixed-format quiz checking core robotics vocabulary and asking learners to apply it.
instructions:
Instructions:

Answer every question in Section 1 before moving to Section 2.
Section 2 has no single right answer — explain your thinking clearly.

items.question: Which part of a robot allows it to sense its environment?
A robot's motor is used to power its movement.
A robot uses a ___ to detect light levels.
Explain in your own words how a robot decides what action to take when its sensor detects an obstacle.
Upload a diagram labeling the main parts of your robot.

name: Robot Sensor Investigation Assignment
description: Learners research a sensor type, then attach and test one on their own robot kit.
instructions:
Instructions:

Complete the research task first.
Then complete the hands-on task using your robot kit.
Submit all files requested for each task.

items.question: Research two different types of sensors used in robotics and describe what each one does.
Attach a sensor to your robot kit and test that it responds correctly to changes in its environment.

name: Line-Following Robot Project
description: A multi-week build project where learners design, build, and program a robot that follows a line on the floor.
instructions:
Instructions:

Work through Planning before you start building.
Use the Build & Test tasks to guide your work sessions.
Submit each deliverable as you complete it.
overview: Design, build, and program a robot that can follow a line on the floor using sensors and motors. Learners will plan their approach, build the robot, and iteratively test it against a practice track.

items.question: Sketch and describe your planned robot design, including sensor placement.
Build your robot and test it on the practice track.

deliverables.name: Design Sketch
deliverables.description: A labeled diagram of your robot design, including where each sensor is placed.

deliverables.name: Working Robot Demo
deliverables.description: A short video demonstrating your robot following the line on the practice track.

milestones.name: Design Approved
milestones.description: Teacher reviews and signs off on your design sketch before building starts.

milestones.name: First Successful Test Run
milestones.description: Robot completes the practice track without going off the line.

name: Robotics Lab Skills Observation
description: An observation checklist used during robotics lab sessions to assess collaboration, communication, and hands-on build skills.
instructions:
Instructions:

Observe the learner during a robotics lab session.
Rate each item based on what you directly observe.
Use the notes item to record anything noteworthy that isn't covered above.

indicators.text: Shares tools and materials with teammates
Communicates ideas clearly during group work
Stays focused and on-task during build time
